S
TUNNING HAUL AS MARKETS FALL!
Bangladesh has been the market of the moment as Chattogram Choppers take in a stunning haul
Ma in market.
of vessels at the bidding tables this week, including three LNGs, and several handymax bulkers at
the best market levels available at the moment, which also stand well above the regional
competition and well ahead of expectations from the Chattogram market even in just the last week.
After a barren few weeks at the empty bidding tables and near empty port reports on several
occasions, the recent past and moreover, this week was compellingly more productive for the local
market as MISC committed three more LNGs to local buyers as the PUTERI MUTIARA SATU
(28,804 LDT), the PUTERI ZAMRUD SATU (28,858 LDT), and the PUTERI FIRU SATU (28,805
LDT) all were committed at over USD 11 million each, basis an ‘as is' Malaysia delivery. In other
sales, two handymax bulkers were committed to local buyers as the OCEAN AMAZING (7,075
Barren recently.
LDT) was fixed for USD 300/LT LDT, also on an ‘as is' Jakarta basis, but under tow. Finally, the
WANTONG SUMMER (8,196 LDT) was finalized for an appreciably firm USD 443/LT LDT.
And as ship owners and ship recyclers were just about fed-up with the overall state of the ship
industry since 2022, Donnie delivered madness and surprising movements in the sub-continent
that even helped local recyclers take delivery of a new 27K LDT LNG, despite an Ozempic
anchorage this week. A thinning anchorage wasn't the only surprise awaiting local recyclers
through the course of the week as Taka plummeted a massive 0.56% against the U.S. Dollar and
Sale s glut.
ended the week at BDT 122.73. Local steel plate

Economically however, the effects of war are starting to hit the nation as the local government has
started rationing fuel delivery at local gas stations that is now leading to long lines and panic at the
pumps. Schools, colleges, and universities have all been reportedly shut down for the time being.
Fertilizer factories have ceased operations as electric load shedding (resulting in power losses) has
become a regular cycle, on that's going to wreak havoc with incoming summer months. The effects
on domestic ship recycling yards are reportedly even more dire as fuel quantities needed to operate
Just stay home?
yard machiner ies a nd motor-driven equipment have reduced operating hours. Local trucking
delays in shifting recycled ship's steels out of yards has resulted in a 50% decline of output as
trucking companies themselves are restricting their fleets from traveling longer distances. Inflation
is back up on the rise as it wrapped February up at 9.13% (up from 8.58 in Jan). Seem only thing
left when the monsoons arrive is for people to just stay home.

The Indian market remains price-positioned a long way away from its Bangladeshi competition (who

generally weathered international storms and events with comparative ease, given, logically, its far
greater economy.
Yet, all is far from well, with a sprinkle of good news thrown into the mix. To start, the rumored

over recent months.

PAKISTAN
M
ORE THAN JUST THE MUSIC!
Pakistan as a nation is facing up to more than just the music over the last few weeks as
Empty handed. economically, politically, and from a ship-recycling standpoint, it's all gone a bit Willy Wonky. To
start, on the international arena, Pakistani intelligence, ISI, is in a full public conflict against the
Afghani Taliban, who it claims are being funded by India to engage in terrorism against Pakistan.
While that is ongoing, the U.S. and Israeli war against Iran is seeing some fallout in Pakistan, given
that the nations are neighbors, and violent pro-Iranian factions in Pakistan have been targeting
Buyers ready to acquire U.S. facilities in the country, with deaths being reported as a result.
On the ship-recycling front, things continue a la Ozempic as another empty-handed week at the
bidding tables for Gadani was instead spent witnessing all of the market tonnage heading to

ongoing, the U.S. and Israeli war against Iran is seeing some fallout in Pakistan, given
that the nations are neighbors, and violent pro-Iranian factions in Pakistan have been targeting
Buyers ready to acquire U.S. facilities in the country, with deaths being reported as a result.
On the ship-recycling front, things continue a la Ozempic as another empty-handed week at the
bidding tables for Gadani was instead spent witnessing all of the market tonnage heading to
Chattogram shores, at rates far beyond the reach of offers tabled by the Gadani Gang, and despite
Regional upheaval.
demand still being ripe for the time being, having taken several larger LDT bulkers so far this year.
But like its sub-continent brethren, local fundamentals here remained in a pickle as, on the one
hand, the Pakistani Rupee slid 0.48% and called it a week at a still-strong PKR 279.20 against the
U.S.$, whilst local steel plate prices were the only rebels of the fundamentals as they climbed over
USD 6.50/ton and wrapped things up at nearly USD 599/Ton - the highest margins of the industry.
The logical cessation of inflow of steel scrap from Iran during these trying times will likely help
this key fundamental deliver fair winds in the short term and help local pricing.
De mand good. But with a war-riddled geographic region and an HKC development chart-sheet that's still teething
at best, Gadani's waterfront will continue to see tonnage arrive in squirts every other month - and
with the way 2025, and even 2024, has been for this market, it seems 2026 will likely be no different,
especially as domestic inflation itself surged to 7% last month (up from 5.8% in January).
Like a handicapped father's unwitting dance at his daughter's wedding, Gadani's delivery
schedule seems trapped in a similar fate.

Eid al-Fitr has arrived to greet the world's ship recycling markets this week, and as is tradition, it came bearing the giftof silence. From Chattogram to Gadani, Alang to Aliağa, the holy festival has pressed pause on an industry that was already beginning to feel the compounding weight of war, currency volatility, and a stubborn absence of fresh tonnage offerings.
The backdrop to this year's Eid break is, to put it charitably, anything but restful. The ongoing conflict in the Middle East, which has now stretched into its third week following the U.S.-Israeli military campaign against Iran, continues to send tremors through global energy and freight markets in equal measure. Brent crude briefly pierced USD 119/barrel on Thursday following Iranian retaliatory strikes on Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG facility, before retreating to settle at USD 108.65/barrel after Netanyahu signalled Israel was helping reopen the Strait of Hormuz. The week's close above USD 100/barrel for the sixth consecutive session shows just how embedded this new price floor has become.
For ship recyclers, the arithmetic of this war continues to deliver perverse outcomes. Higher oil prices logically translate into higher freight earnings for trading vessels, which in turn means owners are less inclined to sell tonnage for recycling when their ageing units are suddenly pulling respectable income. The Baltic Dry Index closed at 2,057 today, down 7 points on the session but still firm relative to the sub-1,600 lows seen in January, another nail in the coffin for those hoping for a surge of demolition candidates before the monsoon season arrives in June.
The U.S. Dollar continued its reign across all ship recycling destination currencies this week. The Indian Rupee, Pakistani Rupee, Bangladeshi Taka, and Turkish Lira all weakened against the USD, adding inflationary pressure to domestic steel pricing that is already under strain from disrupted supply chains. For recyclers whose revenues are denominated in USD but whose cost bases run in local currency, this would ordinarily be welcome news. Instead, the combination of reduced tonnage supply and unsettled buyer sentiment has meant that the currency advantage has delivered little in the way of increased appetite.
If there is any comfort to be found this week, it is that the Eid holiday provides a natural pause during which yards in both Bangladesh and Pakistan can press forward on their Hong Kong Convention compliance upgrades. With the HKC now in force and

revenues are denominated in USD but whose cost bases run in local currency, this would ordinarily be welcome news. Instead, the combination of reduced tonnage supply and unsettled buyer sentiment has meant that the currency advantage has delivered little in the way of increased appetite.
If there is any comfort to be found this week, it is that the Eid holiday provides a natural pause during which yards in both Bangladesh and Pakistan can press forward on their Hong Kong Convention compliance upgrades. With the HKC now in force and inspections increasingly rigorous, any time away from active purchasing is time that can be redirected toward certification.

festival.
The Bangladeshi Taka continued to lose ground against the U.S. Dollar this week, slipping a further
Tak a losing ground. 8 basis points to close at BDT 122.26, its weakest level against the Dollar in 2026. For recyclers
buying tonnage in dollars and selling recycled steel in Taka, this persistent currency erosion is a
structural headache that holiday cheer does little to address. Local steel plate prices nudged
upward marginally, finishing the week at approximately USD 511/ton, though this modest
improvement was largely driven by supply anxiety rather than genuine demand strength, as the
war's impact on regional steel flows continues to introduce unpredictability into procurement
chains.
The two OFAC-sanctioned VLCCs that have been idling at OPL Chattogram continue to attract
No OFAC. considerable industry attention, though not of the productive variety. Neither vessel has yet
received the No Objection Certificate required for entry into port limits, and the BSBRA has made
clear it will not facilitate their delivery. The episode serves as a pointed reminder of the risks that
some cash buyers appear willing to accept in pursuit of margin, and of the reputational damage
that can flow to the wider market when international sanctions are visibly flouted at the anchorage.
Looking ahead, the post-Eid period will be the true test. If Bangladesh can couple its pricing
Supply issues. advantage with faster central bank approvals on Letters of Credit, a bottleneck that has frustrated

Alang's yards observed Eid with a quiet that belied the underlying anxiety of the week. India's ship
recycling community finds itself watching two crises unfold simultaneously, one external, one C rises unfold.
domestic, and neither is particularly conducive to bold purchasing decisions.
On the external front, the war in the Middle East has now directly disrupted India's two primary crude
oil supply relationships. Iranian supplies are effectively suspended for the foreseeable future following Gas shortage?
the closure of Hormuz to unchaperoned transit, while Russian oil, already subject to the price cap
architecture, faces fresh U.S. secondary sanctions pressure that has made Indian refiners increasingly
cautious about their exposure. The practical result has been a sharp rise in the landed cost of energy
across India's industrial base, with gas shortage rumours at Alang, though firmly denied by the
BSBRA's Indian equivalent, adding a layer of operational uncertainty that recyclers could do without.
Domestically, the Indian Rupee extended its weakening trend against the U.S. Dollar, closing the week
at approximately Rs. 92.40, a new low for 2026 and a level that has rattled sentiment among Alang's R upee worries.
more cautious buyers. Local steel plate prices remained volatile, trading in a range between USD 418
and USD 422/ton through the week before settling at approximately USD 422/ton by Thursday. The
intra-week swings, driven in part by speculation about steel scrap import disruption from war-affected
regions, are making it difficult for Alang recyclers to set offer levels with any confidence, as the
underlying cost basis shifts beneath their feet.

notable is the underlying sentiment heading into the break, which is one of cautious optimism that
represents a meaningful improvement from the despair that gripped Gadani for much of the past
eighteen months.

was therefore entirely expected rather than concerning. What is more
notable is the underlying sentiment heading into the break, which is one of cautious optimism that
represents a meaningful improvement from the despair that gripped Gadani for much of the past
eighteen months.
Buyers ready to acquire Local steel plate prices held firm at USD 591.50/ton through the week, a remarkable steadiness in
the context of regional turbulence, while the Pakistani Rupee held stable at PKR 279.15 against the
U.S. Dollar, effectively flat week-on-week and representing a rare island of currency calm in a sea
of depreciation across the sub-continent. For recyclers buying tonnage in dollars, this stability is a
meaningful operational advantage.
The geopolitical dimension of the current conflict has, counterintuitively, presented Gadani with a
Geopolitics. modest opportunity. Regional tonnage owners, particularly those with tankers that were trading
in Gulf waters and now find themselves either displaced or facing prohibitive war risk insurance,
are beginning to consider Gadani as a potential end destination. Pakistan's geographic proximity
to the Gulf, combined with yard availability and competitive pricing relative to Bangladesh, makes
Gadani a plausible landing point for some of this stranded tonnage.
Prime Green Recyclers, Pakistan's first HKC-certified yard along with Salams International and
Al Hamza BV HKC! now this week, in further encouraging news, Al Hamza Commodities as the first BV Class
Certified HKC yard in Gadani, continues to attract serious interest from owners who need a
compliant South Asian alternative to Alang facilities. Additional Gadani yards are reportedly
within two to three months of completing their own HKC upgrade journeys, a development that,
when it materialises, will meaningfully expand Pakistan's addressable market for green demolition
work.
Factions opposed to U.S. and Israeli actions in Iran have continued to demonstrate in major
Pakistani cities, and isolated incidents near diplomatic facilities have been reported. These tensions
add a layer of country risk that some international owners remain sensitive to, even where
recycling yard operations themselves are unaffected. The situation bears monitoring as the conflict
develops.

Turkey had a brief/busy run, and now it is quiet again. After a period of surprising activity in the
opening weeks of 2026, Aliağa has returned to a state of dormancy that has become familiar. Eid al-
Fitr holidays, running from March 19 through 23, have provided convenient cover for what was
TRY challenges.
already shaping up to be a subdued week, and no sales or significant enquiries were reported across
the entirety of the period. The Turkish Lira continued its seemingly inexorable slide against the U.S.
Dollar, trading at TRY 44.32 per Dollar this week, a fresh record low. While a weaker Lira
theoretically makes Turkish steel more competitive in export markets, the domestic recycling sector
operates primarily on local scrap economics, and a depreciating currency simply inflates the input
costs that Aliağa yards face when bidding for international tonnage priced in dollars.
The Ege Çelik delisting from the EU's approved ship recycling facility register, which saw the yard
removed following an inspection that cited insufficient pre-cleaning and the absence of an
impermeable shoreline surface, continues to cast a shadow over Aliağa's regulatory credibility. While
Ege Çelik has submitted corrective plans to the relevant authorities, the reputational damage of a
public delisting is not easily repaired, and European owners considering Turkish yards for their EU-
flagged vessels will need to satisfy themselves that alternatives on the approved list can absorb their
requirements. Import steel prices at Aliağa continued their modest weekly corrections, with HMS
1&2 scrap edging lower by approximately USD 4/ton, reflecting both the holiday slowdown in
purchasing activity and the broader uncertainty about steel demand in the construction sector that
has been weighing on Turkish scrap economics since the start of Q1.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The Indian ship recycling market remained at a standstill this week, with sentiment
coming under pressure from the extraordinary increase in the USD/INR index and the
lack of corresponding improvement in local steel prices.
Market participants are also closely watching developments surrounding the
government's ship recycling scheme, where active work on the broader structure
remains ongoing and a standardized process is expected to be introduced in due
course.
Overall sentiment remains negative; however, the scarcity of available tonnage has
continued to attract some keen buyers who may still be willing to pay reasonable
levels, even as profitability remains under question.
BANGLADESH
Activity in the Bangladesh ship recycling market improved this week, with buyers
showing increased urgency to secure vessels ahead of the upcoming budget and
particularly targeting units that can be delivered before the new measures take effect.
Prices have remained firm, although the local market has not fully moved in line with
the stronger levels being offered by buyers, reflecting expectations that market
conditions may turn more favorable in the near term.
Sentiment in the region has also been supported by the presence of a newly elected
business-friendly political environment, which has added a more positive undertone to
the market.
PAKISTAN
The Pakistani ship recycling market remained largely unchanged from the previous
week, with sentiment showing little movement overall.
Market activity was limited as Eid holidays continued over the past few days, with most
participants away from the market during this period.
Looking ahead, a clearer direction may begin to emerge in the coming week as normal
activity resumes.

With participants expected to return after the holidays, there is potential for improved
activity and sentiment in the week ahead.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The Indian ship recycling market witnessed reduced buying interest this week,
primarily due to the significant strengthening of the USD, making imports more
expensive for local buyers.
Market participants remain cautious as the strong dollar continues to weigh on
sentiment and purchasing appetite.
The DASR (Document of Authorization for Ship Recycling) process remains ongoing,
with completion expected within the next two to three months.
BANGLADESH
The Bangladesh ship recycling market remained firm this week, with healthy demand
observed across available tonnage.
Sentiment has been supported by the evolving political landscape, which has brought a
more positive outlook to the market.
Overall conditions appear stable; however, there are no major new developments to
report at this stage.
PAKISTAN
The Pakistani ship recycling market remained largely unchanged, with no fresh
developments compared to previous weeks.
Market activity continues to be subdued, with no vessels reportedly offered to the
destination.
Additionally, the market is not yet fully operational, contributing to the lack of activity
and momentum.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
The Turkish ship recycling market showed some improvement in underlying
fundamentals this week.
Import prices increased by approximately USD 8, while domestic steel prices improved
by around USD 10.
Despite these positive indicators, vessel prices have yet to reflect any upward
movement. Market participants remain watchful, with expectations that the coming
week may provide clearer direction.

As the world's ship recycling markets emerge from the Eid al-Fitr holidays, they do so into an environment thatcontinues to generate more questions than answers. The post-holiday reopening, always anticipated as a potential catalyst for pent-up activity, has instead delivered something more characteristic of the broader 2026 narrative: confusion. Steel plate prices played opposite roles in Pakistan and India this week, as Indian levels recorded an impressive jump while Pakistani levels continued to bleed, declining non-stop over the course of the last month now. Bangladeshi and Chinese levels continued to flatline at the bottom of the pool, offering little directional signal to a market already starved of pricing clarity.
Even the U.S. Dollar, which has otherwise been a reliable source of sub-continent pressure this year, chose this week to remain undecided, making firm improvements against some recycling currencies while losing ground against others. The mixed signals have left buyers and sellers alike struggling to anchor their negotiating positions, and the result has been another week in which market activity remained well below the levels needed to make meaningful inroads into the tonnage backlog that has been building throughout Q1.
Bangladesh has emerged from the Eid break in a surprisingly bullish frame of mind, likely on the back of a few hungry recyclers whose yards have cleared sufficient space to welcome fresh tonnage. This is a noteworthy shift from the cautious posture that defined the Chattogram market through the holiday period. India and Pakistan, by contrast, struggled to keep pace despite the end of the holy month, hampered by the persistent absence of workable tonnage and the ongoing uncertainty around steel economics that has made confident bidding difficult to sustain.
The broader macro context remains unchanged and unresolved. The Middle East conflict continues to keep freight earnings elevated, discouraging owners from sending ageing tonnage for recycling. The Baltic Dry Index has yet to give meaningful ground. Oil remains firmly above USD 100/barrel, reinforcing the perverse arithmetic of a market where higher energy prices translate into longer trading lives for the very vessels that ship recyclers need. As Week 13 closes and Q2 begins, the industry finds itself asking the same question it has asked for most of the past year: when will the tonnage arrive in sufficient quantity to match the appetite that recyclers, particularly in Bangladesh, are beginning to demonstrate?

BANGLADESH
B
ANGLA BOON!
Bangladesh enters the post-Eid period with an appetite that has caught more than a few observers
Ap petite good.
off guard. After weeks of cautious, holiday-dampened sentiment, Chattogram's recyclers appear
to have used the festival break to clear inventory, settle financing, and sharpen their pencils for the
fresh tonnage hunt that typically follows the Eid pause. The mood at the waterfront is meaningfully
more engaged than it was heading into the holiday, with reports of several buyers actively seeking
units in the days following the resumption of normal operations.
VLs linger.
The two OFAC

.
off guard. After weeks of cautious, holiday-dampened sentiment, Chattogram's recyclers appear
to have used the festival break to clear inventory, settle financing, and sharpen their pencils for the
fresh tonnage hunt that typically follows the Eid pause. The mood at the waterfront is meaningfully
more engaged than it was heading into the holiday, with reports of several buyers actively seeking
units in the days following the resumption of normal operations.
VLs linger.
The two OFAC-sanctioned VLCCs that have been idling at OPL Chattogram since before the Eid
break remain unresolved. As highlighted in last week's edition, both vessels fell afoul of USDN
rules and have become victims of OFAC sanctions, with no prospects of a final resting place on the
immediate horizon. The episode has sharpened due diligence practices among cash buyers and
recyclers, who are now increasingly vigilant in their vetting of proposed tonnage, particularly units
that may have changed hands through channels connected to sanctioned parties. Reports suggest
there are still a handful of similarly suspect units in cash buyer hands awaiting introduction to the
market. Given the fate of the Chattogram duo, how these pending and future dealings will
Stee l flat. eventually transpire remains very much to be seen.
Bangladeshi and Chinese steel levels continued to flatline this week, providing little fundamental
support for any dramatic upward repricing of recycling offers. The Letter of Credit bottleneck that
has frustrated transactions in recent weeks has not yet fully resolved, though there are indications
that central bank approvals are beginning to move more smoothly in the post-holiday
Yearning supply. environment. If that pipeline clears, and if the tonnage that Bangladesh's hungry recyclers are
seeking actually materialises, the market could yet see a pre-monsoon burst of activity before the
June rains close the window. Whether tonnage availability will cooperate remains the unanswered
question.

INDIA
O
N THE LOOKOUT!
India delivered one of the more surprising data points of the week in the form of a sharp upward move
in local steel plate prices, an impressive jump that has provided Alang's recyclers with at least a S teel firms.
temporary boost to their pricing confidence. The move is a welcome reversal from the prolonged
softness that has characterised Indian steel economics for much of Q1, though veterans of the Alang
market will be cautious about extrapolating too much from a single week's movement in a market that
has demonstrated a pronounced tendency to give back gains almost as quickly as it makes them.
Lacking tonnage.
The deeper structural challenge at Alang remains unchanged: there is simply not enough workable
tonnage arriving at the waterfront. Strong freight earnings, elevated oil prices, and owners' continued
preference for keeping aging vessels in service rather than sending them for recycling have combined
to leave Alang's anchorage thin and its yards underutilised. The arrival of a handful of smaller regional
general cargo vessels, largely units displaced from war-affected trading routes, has provided some
activity, but the larger LDT units that Alang's recyclers need to generate meaningful revenue remain
stubbornly absent from the demolition pipeline.
India's strongest competitive position continues to be its HKC compliance infrastructure. With more
than 110 yards now holding valid Statements of Compliance, Alang is the only sub-continent O ver 100 HKC.
destination capable of absorbing significant volumes of regulated European and Japanese tonnage at
scale. GMS has continued its public advocacy for the inclusion of qualified Indian yards on the EU's
approved recycling facility register, noting that continued exclusion appears driven by political
considerations rather than compliance standards. Whether that advocacy translates into regulatory
progress in Brussels before the next wave of EU-flagged vessels reaches end-of-life will be a defining EU questions.

PAKISTAN
S
TILL KEEN!
Pakistan produced something of a surprise this week, taking in more vessels than India despite a
See king tonnage. clear overall decline in the number of arrivals and deliveries across the sub-continent board, and
despite the ongoing deterioration in local steel plate prices that has become Gadani's most
persistent structural headache. Pakistani steel levels have declined non-stop over the last month, a
trend that sits in stark contrast to the sharp upward move recorded in India this week and that
continues to compress the margin available to Gadani's recyclers on each unit they acquire.
Buyers ready to acquire
The tonnage arrivals, modest as they are in absolute terms, nonetheless represent a meaningful
H K C gains. signal of continued buyer engagement at Gadani, a market that was effectively written off by much
of the industry as recently as twelve months ago. The combination of three new HKC certified
yards, the geographic advantage of proximity to Gulf tonnage displaced by the ongoing conflict,
and the relative availability of yard space has made Gadani a viable landing point for owners who
need a compliant, accessible South Asian alternative to Alang.
The Pakistani Rupee's recent steadiness, holding broadly flat after weeks of depreciation pressure,
Rupee steady. provides at least a temporary floor under Gadani's economics. Should steel prices stabilise and
additional HKC certifications materialise in the coming weeks, Pakistan's improved position in the
regional competitive landscape could translate into a more sustained flow of tonnage. For now, the
market remains a work in progress, better than it was, not yet where it needs to be.

[Page 5]
GMS Weekly Page 5 of 8
TURKEY
S
TEADY BUT SILENT!
Turkey ends Week 13 in much the same position it has occupied for the better part of the year:
structurally disadvantaged on price, watching the sub-continent capture what little tonnage is
available, and waiting for a market shift that shows no sign of arriving on the immediate horizon.
Disadvantaged.
Aliaga's yards continued to operate at subdued levels through the post-Eid week, with no significant
sales or major new enquiries reported across the period.
The Turkish Lira's continuing depreciation against the U.S. Dollar remains the defining constraint on
Turkish recycling economics. At indicative levels of USD 265-285/LDT depending on vessel type,
Aliaga cannot realistically compete with sub-continent pricing for mainstream commercial tonnage.
The domestic steel scrap market, which ultimately sets the ceiling on what Turkish recyclers can
offer, continues to reflect sluggish demand from the construction sector and the broader uncertainty
that has characterised the Turkish economic environment through Q1.
Turkey's structural advantage in the specialist segment, EU-approved yards able to receive EU-
flagged vessels under the Ship Recycling Regulation, continues to provide a thin but durable revenue
stream for the handful of facilities that hold approved status. The Ege Çelik delisting and the
reputational overhang it created for Aliaga's regulatory credibility has not helped, and European
owners considering Turkish yards for their regulated tonnage will continue to apply careful scrutiny.
For the broader Turkish market, the question of when it next becomes genuinely competitive for
international recycling tonnage remains open, and the answer, for now, does not appear to be this
week.

Week 14 opens the second quarter of 2026 under the same structural pressures that defined Q1, and then some.The Iran war continues to dominate the macro agenda, with Brent crude holding above USD 109/barrel for a seventh consecutive week and the Strait of Hormuz remaining effectively constrained. Geopolitical risk has overtaken supply-demand fundamentals as the primary price driver in energy markets, and ship recycling is feeling the impact from both sides, as higher oil keeps aging vessels trading while a volatile USD makes confident bidding difficult to sustain.
India delivered the week's most notable currency development, as the rupee rebounded sharply from its record low of 94.42 to 92.73 following intervention measures by the Reserve Bank of India, including curbs on forward transactions, limits on banks' FX positions, and restrictions on corporate rebooking of cancelled contracts. Pakistan and Bangladesh saw only marginal currency movement, while the Turkish lira weakened further to a fresh record low of 44.59, continuing a prolonged depreciation trend that is steadily eroding Aliağa's competitiveness for international tonnage.
Even the U.S. Dollar, which has otherwise been a consistent source of pressure on sub-continent markets this year, sent mixed signals again this week, strengthening against the Turkish lira while easing against the Indian rupee. The INR's recovery offers some support to Alang by improving USD-denominated bidding capacity, though the sustainability of this move remains uncertain if oil prices continue to rise and capital outflows resume. These mixed signals continue to cloud price discovery, leaving buyers and sellers struggling to anchor positions and keeping overall market activity subdued, with little progress made on clearing the tonnage backlog that has built up over the course of Q1.
Bangladesh remains the standout positive in the region. The post-Eid momentum seen last week has carried into Week 14, with Chattogram recyclers actively seeking tonnage and maintaining the most engaged sentiment across the sub-continent. India and Pakistan, by contrast, continue to operate cautiously, constrained by limited supply and ongoing uncertainty in steel fundamentals that continues to weigh on bidding confidence.
The broader macro backdrop remains largely unchanged. The Middle East conflict continues to support elevated freight earnings, discouraging owners from recycling older vessels. The Baltic Dry Index has shown only marginal softening, easing to 2,031, while oil remains firmly above USD 100/barrel for a seventh consecutive week. The result is a market where higher energy prices continue to extend vessel trading

contrast, continue to operate cautiously, constrained by limited supply and ongoing uncertainty in steel fundamentals that continues to weigh on bidding confidence.
The broader macro backdrop remains largely unchanged. The Middle East conflict continues to support elevated freight earnings, discouraging owners from recycling older vessels. The Baltic Dry Index has shown only marginal softening, easing to 2,031, while oil remains firmly above USD 100/barrel for a seventh consecutive week. The result is a market where higher energy prices continue to extend vessel trading lives, delaying the flow of tonnage into recycling yards.
As Week 14 settles into Q2, the industry is left asking a familiar question: when will tonnage begin to arrive in sufficient volume to meet the growing appetite from recyclers, particularly in Bangladesh?

UNGRY AFTER THE HOLIDAY
The bullishness that characterised Chattogram's return from the Eid break shows no sign of fading
Mo mentum holds.
as Week 14 opens. Bangladesh's recyclers remain among the most actively engaged in the region,
with yard space continuing to clear and appetite for fresh tonnage undiminished. The mood at the
waterfront is more constructive than it has been at any point in Q1, and the Letter of Credit pipeline,
which has been a persistent drag on transaction velocity, is beginning to move more smoothly as
post-holiday central bank approvals gather pace. Bangladesh continues to hold firmer ground in
terms of prices offered for recycling candidates than India, maintaining the competitive edge that
has defined the sub-continent's pricing hierarchy through the opening quarter.
The two OFAC-sanctioned VLCCs idling at OPL Chattogram remain unresolved and are now
Compliance tightens.
entering their third week of limbo. No pathway to a final resting place has emerged, and the
heightened due diligence culture they have prompted across the cash buyer community shows no
sign of easing. Reports continue to suggest a small number of similarly suspect units remain in cash
buyer hands awaiting introduction to the market. Each week that passes without resolution of the
Chattogram duo reinforces the caution that buyers and recyclers are now applying to any tonnage
with opaque ownership or transaction history. It is a compliance hangover that will outlast the
specific vessels that caused it.

IRMER GROUND
Pakistan enters Week 14 on firmer commercial footing than at any point during Q1. The steel plate
Fir m footing. jump recorded at the end of March, rising from PKR 168,000 to PKR 175,000 per ton, has held into
the new quarter, with pricing consolidating near PKR 175,000 and the USD equivalent settling
around USD 627 per ton. The Pakistani Rupee has remained broadly stable at 279.07, reinforcing
this improvement and marking a clear shift from the prolonged weakness that weighed on
Gadani's economics through much of March. Market indications now place Pakistan ahead of
India in pricing terms, reestablishing Gadani as the sub-continent's second most competitive
Buyers ready to acquire
recycling destination.
Structural improvements are also becoming more visible. Three HKC-certified yards are now
Ba ck in play. operational, with further certifications expected in the coming weeks. Combined with proximity
to Gulf-sourced tonnage influenced by ongoing regional tensions, Gadani is increasingly
positioning itself as a practical alternative when Alang faces limited inflow. The current pool of
recycling candidates, including medium-sized dry bulk, LPG, and offshore units, aligns well with
Gadani's available yard capacity and improving compliance profile.
At current levels, the gap between indicative sentiment and executable pricing appears narrower
Supply decides. than it has been in recent months. With steel holding firm and the PKR stable, recyclers are
operating on a more predictable cost base than earlier in the year. The key variable now is supply.
Whether owners choose to release tonnage at prevailing levels, and whether regional dynamics
continue to direct units toward Pakistan, will determine how sustainable this improvement proves
to be. The market

TILL ALONE AT THE BOTTOM TABLE
Turkey enters Q2 with the Turkish Lira weakening further to a record low of 44.59 against the U.S.
Lira slides.
Dollar, extending a depreciation trend that has now run for most of the past year and reflects a
decline of over 17% during that period. The Central Bank of Turkey has intervened heavily,
reportedly selling more than USD 8 billion in reserves during March, while also halting its rate-
cutting cycle in response to inflationary pressures driven by rising energy costs. These measures,
however, have provided little relief to Aliaga's recyclers. The continued weakening of the lira directly
compresses the USD value of local steel prices, widening the gap between Turkish offers and sub-
continent levels.

Outpriced again.

uncertainty and elevated energy prices continue to weigh on the domestic economy, and activity
levels reflect this pressure, with no significant sales or major new enquiries reported during the week.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
Indian recyclers remain keen to acquire fresh tonnage, with currency movements
offering some support to buyers, while softer steel prices continue to weigh on
sentiment.
Breakers are still showing interest, though bidding levels are not strong enough to
match those currently being offered by their counterparts in Bangladesh.
The ceasefire has also slowed buying activity, with recyclers taking a more measured
approach for the time being.
The first few yards that had applied have now received DASR, with Priya Blue
Industries being one of them.
BANGLADESH
The Chittagong market remains firm and is expected to stay so through the end of
May.
Buyers are actively securing tonnage in anticipation of potential unfavorable changes
to duties in the upcoming June budget, which has supported current demand.
A few recent sales have been concluded at healthy levels, reflecting the strength in the
market.
At the same time, sentiment remains mixed, with some breakers bidding aggressively
and pushing prices higher than the previous week, while the ceasefire has slightly
softened the overall mood.
PAKISTAN
The market is becoming keener by the day for the purchase of fresh tonnage due to
the ongoing conflict.
The stoppage of imported steel, including previously unofficial inflows, is supporting
local market conditions for now. Following the ceasefire, it remains to be seen how
long this situation will persist.
For the time being, the market is expected to remain relatively buoyant.
Foreign exchange issues continue to persist, while longer payment timelines remain a
challenge for buyers.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
The Turkish market remained largely unchanged this week, with no notable
movement in pricing or sentiment.
While some increase in import activity and local movement had been observed in the
previous week, no such momentum was seen this week.
Overall, the market continues to hold at previous levels, with neither a clear rise nor
fall evident for the time being.

The single most consequential development of Week 15 came out of Washington, not the recycling yards. President Trump'sdecision to delay planned strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure by five days, alongside comments describing talks with Iran as "productive," triggered the sharpest oil reversal since the conflict began. Brent, which had held above USD 109/barrel through Q1 and into early Q2, fell to near USD 101/barrel by week's end, an almost 8% drop in a matter of hours. WTI followed, settling around USD 90/barrel. Tehran denied any negotiations even as Washington insisted talks were ongoing, highlighting just how fragile the situation remains. The Strait of Hormuz is still constrained, but markets are now pricing in the possibility of reopening, and that alone has begun to erode the war premium in energy markets.
The Baltic Dry Index rose to 2,139 by April 8th, continuing a recovery that began from the March trough and extending to its highest level since early March. Capesize earnings improved as a Brazil-to-China iron ore window opened, with the BCI C3 Brazil-to-Qingdao route briefly breaking above USD 30/metric ton for the first time since July 2024. Panamax and supramax both held gains. The improvement in freight sentiment is notable against the backdrop of the oil move. It suggests that some operators are reading the diplomatic pause as a stabilization signal rather than a durable resolution and are positioning freight accordingly. For ship recycling, a firming BDI is a headwind on the supply side. Vessel earnings continue to provide incentive to trade, not recycle.
Currency markets remained largely stable. The Indian Rupee softened slightly to 93.21, reversing part of last week's recovery, while Pakistan and Bangladesh currencies held steady. The Turkish Lira improved modestly from recent lows but remains structurally weak. A softer Dollar, with the DXY near 99.3, offers some support to sub-continent buyers heading into Q2.
For recyclers, the oil correction is important but not decisive. Lower fuel costs may eventually undermine trading economics for older vessels, but the move is still too recent to trigger immediate recycling flows. Owners are unlikely to react to a single week's volatility. The Q1 backlog of tonnage remains in place, with conditions improving but not yet compelling.
As Week 15 closes, the industry stands at a genuine inflection point, one that was not visible at the start

inent buyers heading into Q2.
For recyclers, the oil correction is important but not decisive. Lower fuel costs may eventually undermine trading economics for older vessels, but the move is still too recent to trigger immediate recycling flows. Owners are unlikely to react to a single week's volatility. The Q1 backlog of tonnage remains in place, with conditions improving but not yet compelling.
As Week 15 closes, the industry stands at a genuine inflection point, one that was not visible at the start of Q2. Whether this moment marks the beginning of supply normalization or merely a temporary reprieve in a geopolitically defined market will determine the character of Q2. Bangladesh has both the financial capacity and the pre-monsoon urgency to absorb tonnage immediately. India and Pakistan are in their strongest structural positions of the year. The question, as it has been since the Iran war began, is when owners will act.

BANGLADESH
T
HE WINDOW NARROWS
Bangladesh enters Week 15 with the sharpest improvement in steel economics seen in the quarter.
Ste el spikes.
Local steel plate prices jumped to BDT 71,000 per ton, the most significant single-week move of
Q1-Q2, translating to a USD equivalent near USD 578/ton at current exchange rates. With the
USD/BDT rate holding steady at 122.70-122.85, barely changed from 122.81-122.91 at the close of
Week 14, the steel price improvement flows directly into offer capacity without the FX offset that
has blunted similar gains in Alang and Aliağa. Bangladesh continues to hold its position as the sub-
continent's highest bidder and its most actively engaged recycling market.
The Letter of Credit pipeline, which served as a persistent friction point on transaction velocity
LC flow improves.
through Q1, continues to clear at an accelerating pace. Post-Eid central bank approvals are
gathering momentum, and the structural transaction bottleneck that defined the opening quarter
is meaningfully less acute entering Week 15. The two OFAC-sanctioned VLCCs at OPL Chattogram
enter their fourth week unresolved. The heightened due diligence culture they have embedded
across the cash buyer community shows no sign of easing, and the compliance shadow they cast
over any tonnage with opaque documentation continues to slow the introduction of otherwise
qualified recycling candidates to the market. A small number of similarly classified units
reportedly remain in cash buyer hands.
Bangladesh's March 2026 CPI eased to 8.71%, down from 9.13% in February, which had been the

highest reading since May 2025. The moderation was driven by food price declines in the post-Eid
period, while non-food inflation edged higher to 9.09%. The relative macro stability provides
continued central bank flexibility on LC approvals. However, the pre-monsoon

edly remain in cash buyer hands.
Bangladesh's March 2026 CPI eased to 8.71%, down from 9.13% in February, which had been the
Pre-monsoon urgency.
highest reading since May 2025. The moderation was driven by food price declines in the post-Eid
period, while non-food inflation edged higher to 9.09%. The relative macro stability provides
continued central bank flexibility on LC approvals. However, the pre-monsoon window is now the
dominant operational constraint. With the June rains approximately seven weeks away, the
pressure to convert appetite into executed transactions is intensifying. The oil pullback, if it signals
even a partial and sustained release of the Q1 tonnage overhang, arrives at precisely the right
moment. Bangladesh has both the financial positioning and the urgency to absorb tonnage rapidly.
The question remains one of supply, not demand.

INDIA
R
UPEE GIVES BACK, SUPPLY STILL ABSENT
India's Week 15 was defined by the partial reversal of the currency gains that had been Week 14's most
positive domestic development. The USD/INR rate moved back to approximately 93.21, retracing a R upee reversal.
portion of the RBI-engineered recovery from the record low of 94.42 recorded in the prior week. The
move aligns with the risk outlined in Week 14, that the INR recovery's durability was conditional on
both oil price behavior and capital flow dynamics, neither of which has been resolved. The oil pullback
is directionally positive for India's import bill, with approximately 70% of crude and 90% of LPG
transiting outside the current Hormuz constraint, but the market is treating the diplomatic pause with
appropriate caution. The rupee has accordingly given back a portion of the intervention-driven gains.
LPG supply disruptions at Alang's steel mills have shown only partial easing. Local steel plate prices
reached INR 41,000 during the week before settling back near INR 40,600, a mid-week improvement Energy

appropriate caution. The rupee has accordingly given back a portion of the intervention-driven gains.
LPG supply disruptions at Alang's steel mills have shown only partial easing. Local steel plate prices
reached INR 41,000 during the week before settling back near INR 40,600, a mid-week improvement Energy drag lingers.
that was not fully sustained, reflecting the uneven recovery in yard-level energy supply. At a USD
equivalent of approximately USD 436/ton, India remains the lowest-priced destination in the sub-
continent. This position reflects neither a lack of appetite nor infrastructure capacity, but a combination
of energy disruption, unfavorable exchange rate dynamics, and the continued absence of adequate
recycling tonnage flow. India's February 2026 CPI of 3.21% confirms that inflation remains well within
the RBI's target band, but the LPG dependency on the Strait of Hormuz remains the key operational
vulnerability. The oil pullback's long-term benefit to Alang hinges on whether the Hormuz situation
resolves in a durable manner.
Structurally, India's strongest differentiator heading into Q2 remains its HKC compliance
infrastructure. With more than 110 yards holding valid Statements of Compliance, Alang retains HKC strength.

PAKISTAN
S
TABILITY IS THE STORY
Pakistan's Week 15 is characterized by one quality that has been notably scarce in the sub-continent
Fir m footing. through Q1: stability. The Pakistani Rupee has held in a narrow 278.58-279.00 range through the
week, essentially unchanged from the 279.07 recorded at the close of Week 14, and local steel plate
prices at PKR 177,000, with a USD equivalent of approximately USD 634/ton, have remained firm
without the volatility seen in India and Bangladesh. The gap between Pakistan and India in pricing
terms, approximately USD 200/ton at current levels, reaffirms Gadani's position as the sub-
continent's second most competitive recycling destination. March 2026 CPI at 7.3% represents a
move above the State Bank's 5-7% target, driven primarily by transport, which rose 12.5% on the
month, and housing costs. This remains structurally manageable for yard economics given the
stable PKR base.
Gadani's structural positioning in a Gulf-disrupted environment continues to provide a
Gu lf proximity. meaningful tailwind. Ongoing regional tensions have maintained a proximity advantage for Gulf-
sourced tonnage that aligns well with Gadani's yard configuration. Three HKC-certified yards are
now operational, with further certifications in the pipeline, expanding Pakistan's compliance
credentials and attracting growing interest from owners seeking alternatives to Alang when Indian
inflow is constrained. The circulating pool of recycling candidates, including medium-sized dry
bulk, LPG, and offshore units, continues to align with Gadani's available capacity and improving
compliance profile.
The one ambiguity introduced by the Week 15 oil move is the potential moderation of Gadani's
Oil risk noted. Gulf proximity advantage. If a broader de-escalation of the Iran conflict takes hold, the regional
dynamics that have been directing tonnage toward Pakistan may ease. For now, this remains a
speculative risk rather than an immediate market reality. Pakistan enters the second half of Q2's
pre-monsoon window on its firmest footing of the year, with predictable cost inputs and a
competitive pricing position. The key variable, supply, is shared equally by all four destinations.
Whether the oil pullback becomes the catalyst that finally releases the Q

The Week 15 thesis that the war premium was cracking has not held.What looked like a diplomatic off-ramp has narrowed into a less convincing outcome. Washington and Tehran are considering a two-week ceasefire extension, but talks are now focused on a temporary memorandum to avoid renewed conflict, not the broader agreement and Hormuz reopening markets had priced in.
Risk remains elevated. The U.S. has imposed a naval blockade on Iranian ports, while Tehran continues to restrict the Strait of Hormuz to non-Iranian traffic. The Department of Defense is deploying 6,000 additional troops, with a further 4,200 expected by month-end. A ten-day Israel-Lebanon ceasefire offers only limited relief. Markets are not stabilising. They are adjusting to a higher plateau of risk.
Brent traded between USD 94.89 and USD 97.06 per barrel, settling near USD 95, down from Week 15's USD 101 close. The driver has shifted from de-escalation hopes to demand concerns after the IEA cut its 2026 consumption forecast. At the same time, U.S. crude inventories posted a 9.13 million barrel draw against expectations of a 154,000-barrel build, the first draw after seven consecutive weekly increases. The physical market is tight, but sentiment remains uncertain, offering no support for a reopening of supply corridors or a shift in recycling dynamics.
Freight is clearer. The Baltic Dry Index extended its rally to nine consecutive sessions, closing at 2,523 on April 16, up 39 points and at its highest level since early December. Capesize rates moved above 3,100 points to a four-month high on Brazil to China iron ore flows, with Panamax and Supramax also gaining. Improving earnings are keeping older bulkers trading, and the expected Q1 tonnage overhang remains withheld.
Currencies and steel are not the drivers. The USD/INR is around 93.46, Pakistan and Bangladesh remain steady, the Turkish Lira has held its recovery, and the Dollar Index remains near 99. Bangladesh steel holds Week 15 gains, while Pakistan edges higher.
With roughly six weeks before the monsoon window closes, sub-continent fundamentals are supportive. Bangladesh leads on pricing and improving LC flows, Pakistan offers stability, and India retains its HKC advantage. Yet across all three, yards are waiting for ships that owners are choosing not to sell.
The Week 15

46, Pakistan and Bangladesh remain steady, the Turkish Lira has held its recovery, and the Dollar Index remains near 99. Bangladesh steel holds Week 15 gains, while Pakistan edges higher.
With roughly six weeks before the monsoon window closes, sub-continent fundamentals are supportive. Bangladesh leads on pricing and improving LC flows, Pakistan offers stability, and India retains its HKC advantage. Yet across all three, yards are waiting for ships that owners are choosing not to sell.
The Week 15 expectation of a tonnage release has shifted. Week 16 shows that even a ceasefire extension can keep vessels trading. As Week 17 approaches, the question is whether supply returns before the monsoon or rolls into a backlog, dependent on the Hormuz outcome.

BANGLADESH
S
IX WEEKS TO MONSOON
Bangladesh enters Week 16 with its structural pricing advantage intact and its pre-monsoon clock
Wi ndow narrows. loudest. Local steel plate prices have held at BDT 71,000 per ton, sustaining the USD 578 to USD
584 per ton USD equivalent established the previous week. The USD/BDT rate has remained steady
in the 122.70 to 122.85 range, unchanged from the close of Week 14 and Week 15, preserving the
direct flow-through of steel strength into offer capacity that continues to distinguish Bangladesh
from Alang and Aliaga. Chattogram holds its position as the sub-continent's highest bidder and
the most actively engaged recycling market heading into what is now a narrowing operational
window. The June rains sit approximately six weeks away. Every week of continued supply
hesitation by owners is now a week subtracted from the practical time available to convert appetite
into executed t ransa ctions before yards are forced to slow beaching cycles.
The Letter of Credit pipeline that defined Q1 friction continues to clear at an accelerating pace.
Compliance lingers. Post-Eid central bank approvals are gathering further momentum entering Week 16, and the
structural transaction bottleneck that characterised the opening quarter is now meaningfully less
acute. The two OFAC-sanctioned VLCCs at OPL Chattogram enter their fifth week unresolved, and
the heightened due diligence culture they have embedded across the cash buyer community
remains firmly in place. The compliance shadow over tonnage with opaque documentation
continues to slow the introduction of otherwise qualified recycling candidates, and there is no
operational development this week that suggests relief. A small number of similarly classified units
are reported to remain in cash buyer hands. The LC situation has improved. The compliance
situation has not.
Bangladesh's March 2026 CPI at 8.71% provides continued central bank flexibility on LC approvals
Supply decides. without introducing fresh macro concerns. Non-food inflation at 9.09% remains elevated, but the
relative macro stability is sufficient to sustain the current transaction environment. The pre-

Bangladesh's March 2026 CPI at 8.71% provides continued central bank flexibility on LC approvals
Supply decides. without introducing fresh macro concerns. Non-food inflation at 9.09% remains elevated, but the
relative macro stability is sufficient to sustain the current transaction environment. The pre-
monsoon window is now the dominant operational constraint, and the week-over-week firming of
the Baltic Dry Index to 2,523 points has made the supply side harder, not easier. The oil softening
of the past ten days has not released the Q1 overhang because owners are no longer treating it as a
stabilisation signal. Bangladesh has the financial positioning, the compliance pathway, the
urgency, and the pricing. What it does not have, and what it cannot create, is the tonnage. The
demand is real. The supply is the problem.

ORMUZ STILL SHUT, RUPEE DRIFTS
India's Week 16 is characterised by the continuation of the currency slide that began at the close of
Week 15. The USD/INR rate has drifted to approximately 93.46, extending the partial reversal of the C urrency tells.
RBI-engineered recovery from the Week 14 record low of 94.42. The Strait of Hormuz remains
effectively closed with the US naval blockade in place, which directly undermines the directional
benefit that the Week 15 oil pullback was supposed to deliver to India's import bill. Approximately
90% of India's LPG continues to transit routes impacted by the Hormuz constraint and approximately
70% of crude is similarly exposed. The intervention capacity of the RBI has not been exhausted, but the
appetite to deploy it further in a market where the underlying geopolitical driver has not been resolved
is visibly diminished. The currency is telling the truth that the oil market is only hinting at.
LPG supply disruptions at Alang's steel mills continue to show only partial easing. Local steel plate
prices have oscillated through the week between INR 40,800 and INR 41,900,

is not a function of insufficient appetite or insufficient infrastructure. It is a direct consequence of the

benefit to Alang remains conditional on a durable opening of the strait, which the current ceasefire
extension does not contemplate, and the US blockade does not permit.

PAKISTAN
S
TABILITY BECOMES ADVANTAGE
Pakistan's Week 16 extends and arguably intensifies the stability theme that defined Week 15. The
Sta bility holds. Pakistani Rupee has held in a narrow range near 278.90 through the week, essentially unchanged
from the close of Week 14 and Week 15, and local steel plate prices at PKR 188,000 per ton have
pushed further above the Week 14 level, translating to a USD equivalent near USD 671 per ton.
This places Gadani at its firmest absolute USD pricing position of the year. The gap to India has
widened modestly to approximately USD 230 per ton at current levels, reaffirming Pakistan's
position as the sub-continent's second-most competitive destination with accelerating momentum.
March 2026 CPI at 7.3% sits above the State Bank's 5% to 7% target, driven primarily by transport
at plus 12.5%

671 per ton.
This places Gadani at its firmest absolute USD pricing position of the year. The gap to India has
widened modestly to approximately USD 230 per ton at current levels, reaffirming Pakistan's
position as the sub-continent's second-most competitive destination with accelerating momentum.
March 2026 CPI at 7.3% sits above the State Bank's 5% to 7% target, driven primarily by transport
at plus 12.5% on the month and housing, but the trajectory remains manageable for yard economics
given the stable PKR base.
The continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the US naval blockade of Iranian ports are, on
Ad vantage builds. balance, reinforcing Gadani's structural positioning rather than eroding it. The speculative risk
noted in Week 15, that a broader de-escalation might moderate Pakistan's Gulf proximity
advantage, has receded with the narrowing of the US-Iran talks from full settlement to a temporary
memorandum. Three HKC-certified yards are now operational with further certifications in the
pipeline, and the pool of recycling candidates consisting of medium-sized dry bulk, LPG, and
offshore units continues to align well with Gadani's available capacity. The compliance profile is
improving. The pricing position is firming. The Gulf logistics advantage is holding. All three
reinforcements are operating simultaneously, which is unusual.
The one remaining unknown for Pakistan is whether the structural position translates into
Timing risk. executed transactions before the regional dynamic shifts. A further extension of the ceasefire, or
any development suggesting a durable resolution of the Hormuz situation, would begin to erode
the proximity premium that has been directing tonnage toward Gadani. For now, this remains a
speculative risk rather than an immediate market reality. Pakistan enters the second half of Q2's
pre-monsoon window on the firmest footing of its year, with predictable cost inputs, expanding
compliance credentials, and a competitive pricing position that is strengthening rather than
weakening. The supply variable is shared equally with Bangladesh and India. Whether the oil
pullback becomes the catalyst that finally releases the Q1 backlog will be the defining question of
the coming weeks, and in that question, Pakistan has perhaps the most to lose if the answer comes
quickly, and the most to consolidate if it does not.

TURKEY
H
OLDING, NOT HEALING
Turkey enters Week 16 having consolidated rather than extended the modest gains of the previous
Lira pauses.
week. The Turkish lira has held near 44.30 against the US Dollar, essentially unchanged from the
Week 15 close of 44.34, with neither fresh downside from the broader geopolitical environment nor
meaningful appreciation from the softer Dollar. The Central Bank of Turkey continues to operate
with constrained flexibility, having sold more than USD 8 billion in reserves during March and
having halted its rate-cutting cycle amid persistent inflation pressures. These dynamics remain
unchanged entering Week 16, and the lira's week should be read as a pause rather than a turn.
March 2026 CPI at 30.87% printed below both 31.53% previously and market expectations of 31.4 %,
Inflation diverges.
with the softer reading driven by slower price growth in food, housing, and education. Transport
costs accelerated to plus 34.35% annually and plus 4.52% on the month as fuel costs filtered through.
Independent estimates remain materially higher, with ENAG at 54.62% and Istanbul Chamber data
at 37.68%. The central bank's 16% year-end target remains formally in place but is increasingly
difficult to reconcile with either the official print trajectory or the independent measurements. None
of this shifts the recycling picture.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
Indian recyclers continue to face challenges in securing fresh tonnage, as higher price
levels offered by Bangladesh are drawing vessels away from the market.
Domestic steel prices remain under pressure, limiting buyers' ability to match
competing regional offers.
The USD has strengthened compared to last week, though volatility persists, reflecting
broader market uncertainty. Overall sentiment remains cautious, with buyers
constrained by unsustainable pricing levels.
Should geopolitical conditions stabilize, further USD strengthening and softer local
prices across destinations may follow.
BANGLADESH
The local market has softened marginally, with prices declining by approximately BDT
200 (in USD terms).
Buyer appetite remains firm, with continued interest in acquiring vessel candidates.
Supply of recyclable tonnage remains constrained due to ongoing geopolitical
uncertainty.
Limited vessel availability is expected to keep the market stable to firm, with near-term
direction dependent on geopolitical developments.
PAKISTAN
Market sentiment remains uncertain, with no recent transactions available to establish
a clear direction.
Buyers continue to show interest in acquiring tonnage; however, they remain unable to
compete with the stronger price levels available in Chittagong, while limited vessel
supply continues to constrain activity.
Local scrap demand is offering some support, particularly as imports from the Middle
East remain disrupted by the ongoing conflict.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
The Turkish market remained broadly stable this week, with no meaningful changes in
pricing or overall sentiment.
Additionally, most yards are currently operating at full capacity, with very limited
appetite for new purchases.
While fundamentals have not deteriorated, there is a clear lack of buying urgency

while limited vessel
supply continues to constrain activity.
Local scrap demand is offering some support, particularly as imports from the Middle
East remain disrupted by the ongoing conflict.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
The Turkish market remained broadly stable this week, with no meaningful changes in
pricing or overall sentiment.
Additionally, most yards are currently operating at full capacity, with very limited
appetite for new purchases.
While fundamentals have not deteriorated, there is a clear lack of buying urgency,
largely due to already full yard inventories and limited capacity to absorb additional
tonnage. Buyers are therefore maintaining a wait-and-see approach, showing
discipline on pricing and only considering units that fit very specific requirements.
